content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 2, 2017

Mary Beth Barber
6052 Northcrest Circle
Carmichael, CA 95608

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-17-223

Dear Ms. Barber:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090.² Because your request seeks general guidance, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.³

Note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the
case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional
advice. In addition, the Fair Political Practices Commission does not advise with respect to past
conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter
apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

As an employee of the California State Library, does the Act limit your participation as a
review team member for the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence, another public
agency, in light of the stipend you will receive for your services?

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Section 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² In addition, your agency may impose restrictions on incompatible activities outside of the Act. You should
consult your agency's counsel regarding any restrictions on incompatible activities your agency may impose.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-17-223
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

The Act does not preclude you from serving as a review team member. The Act does prohibit you from taking part in decisions with a foreseeable and material effect on any financial interest you may have and you would have a financial interest in the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence considering the stipend for your services. However, you are not disqualified from decisions with a foreseeable material effect on this governmental entity, unless there is a unique effect on your interest. (Regulation 18703(c)(7).) Accordingly, the Act does not limit your participation as a review team member because there is no indication that your work as a state employee will have a unique effect on the stipend you will receive.⁴

FACTS

You are a state employee at the California State Library, working in a civil service position as an Associate Government Program Analyst ("AGPA") as the assistant to the state librarian and coordinator for special projects. Previous to your position at the California State Library, you were at the California Arts Council as the communications lead (a Public Information Officer 1) and then as the Special Projects Coordinator (another "AGPA" position). You have an extensive volunteer record in arts and education, and currently serve on the Parent Advisory Committee for the Local Control Accountability Plan for the San Juan Unified School District, a position related to parental involvement in the Local Control Funding Formula for school finance.

In addition to your duties at the California State Library, you put in an application to serve as a Local Control Funding Formula Content Library Review Team Member for the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence, which according to its website is a public agency established pursuant to Education Code Section 52074. Your application has been accepted and you plan to take time off from the California State Library on the dates needed to serve in this capacity. The position as a Local Control Funding Formula Content Library Review Team Member comes with a stipend of $4,000 with an additional $2,000 stipend for your employer (if not-self-employed). The position will also reimburse for travel and provide lodging at mandatory Content Review meetings.

ANALYSIS

There is nothing in the Act that precludes you from serving as a review team member for another public agency notwithstanding any stipend received for your services. The Act's conflict of interest provisions do prohibit you from taking part in governmental decisions in which you have a financial interest. (Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest in a decision only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of the official's immediate family, or on any of the official's interests as specified in Section 87103.

⁴ We note that you have asked that we clarify whether serving as a member of the review board is employment outside of your duties with the California State Library, whether you would be representing the California State Library as a member of the review board, and whether the California State Library could accept the employer stipend. However, we express no opinion regarding these question as they all fall outside of the confines of the Act.

File No. I-17-223
Page No. 3

As pertinent to this request, an official has an interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more received by, or promised to, the official within the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) In this case, it does appear that you will have an interest in the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence as a source of income because you will be receiving a stipend for your services on the review board of $500 or more.⁵ However, Regulation 18703(e)(7) provides that the financial effect on the governmental entity is deemed indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if the official establishes that the decision affects a "governmental entity in which the official has an interest and there is no unique effect on the official's interest."⁶

Generally, your participation as a review team member and involvement in decisions before California State Library that may affect the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence are not restricted because there is no indication that your work as a state employee will have a unique effect on the stipend you will receive. If, however, there is any indication that a decision by the California State Library may affect the stipend you receive from the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence, you should seek additional advice.⁷

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

⁵ While the term 'income' excludes salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal governmental agency (Section 82030(b)(2)), we have advised that compensation from a governmental agency constitutes salary that is exempted from the definition of "income" only when received as an employee or consultant of the agency. (Kohn Advice Letter, No. I-93-294.) The "governmental salary" exclusion does not apply to payments received as an independent contractor.

⁶ Notwithstanding the Act's disqualification rules, the stipend received from the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence is potentially reportable income under the Act. To the extent that your position with the California State Library is a position designated in the California State Library's conflict of interest code, you may be required to report the income on your statement of economic interests (Form 700).

⁷ For example, if the advisory commission may recommend allocating measure funds to pay for, or in a manner that may affect, the community service program's dog training courses, there may be a unique effect on your interest and you should seek additional advice.

As an employee of the California State Library, does the Act limit your participation as a 
review team member for the California Collaborative for Educational Excellence, another public 
agency, in light of the stipend you will receive for your services?2 In addition, your agency may impose restrictions on incompatible activities outside of the Act. You should 
consult your agency's counsel regarding any restrictions on incompatible activities your agency may impose. 
l Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)

from decisions with a foreseeable material effect on this governmental entity, unless there is a 
unique effect on your interest. (Regulation l 8703(c)(7).) Accordingly, the Act does not limit your 
participation as a review team member because there is no indication that your work as a state 
employee will have a unique effect on the stipend you will receive.4

3 We note that you have asked that we clarify whether serving as a member of the review board is employment
